title: unordered_map
description: The long-awaited payoff from Header Files: hash-based key-value pairs, average O(1) lookup, and the classic frequency-counting pattern.
objectives: Understand unordered_map as hash-based key-value storage
Use the same insert/access interface as map
Implement the classic frequency-counting DSA pattern
Choose correctly between map and unordered_map

sections.text: 1. Recap: This Was Foreshadowed Long Ago
Back in Header Files, we previewed unordered_map with a specific note: "This will become extremely important during Hashing and LeetCode." That moment has arrived.

unordered_map stores key-value pairs with unique keys, using hashing internally to give average constant-time insertion and lookup — with no guaranteed ordering, exactly like unordered_set compared to set.

Every detail from map carries over identically: [] both accesses and inserts (including the same silent-creation gotcha for missing keys), count() safely checks existence without inserting, and iteration order is unspecified here — unlike map, there's no sorted-by-key guarantee at all.
4. The Classic DSA Pattern: Frequency Counting

sections.title: Why this works so cleanly
points: freq[c]++ is shorthand for freq[c] = freq[c] + 1;
The first time a character is seen, freq[c] doesn't exist yet — [] silently creates it as 0, then immediately increments it to 1
Every later occurrence of the same character just increments its existing count
'banana' has 3 a's, 2 n's, 1 b — exactly what freq reports

sections.text: This is one of the single most common patterns across DSA and LeetCode problems: count occurrences of something (characters, numbers, words) using an unordered_map, then reason about those counts. The very gotcha that was a danger in map (missing keys silently defaulting) becomes exactly the convenience that makes this one-liner work.

rows: Choose when sorted key order is needed
Choose for pure fast lookup / frequency counting — the common DSA default

sections.title: A tally counter at a turnstile
sections.text: Imagine a tally counter for every distinct visitor ID passing through a turnstile — each new ID starts its own count at 0 automatically, and every repeat visit just clicks that specific counter up by one. Nobody needs the tally sheet alphabetized; they just need the count for any given ID, instantly. That's unordered_map doing frequency counting.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Choosing map when sorted iteration isn't actually needed — unordered_map is typically faster and is the common default for pure lookup/counting tasks.
Forgetting unordered_map's iteration order is unspecified — never rely on it matching insertion order or any particular order.
Not recognizing the freq[c]++ pattern — it relies on [] defaulting missing keys to 0, which is genuinely useful here, unlike the danger case in plain map lookups.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the main difference between map and unordered_map?
questions.answer: map keeps keys automatically sorted with O(log n) operations; unordered_map has no ordering guarantee but typically achieves average O(1) operations via hashing.

questions.question: How would you count the frequency of each character in a string using unordered_map?
questions.answer: Iterate the string and increment freq[c] for each character c — [] automatically creates a missing key with a default value of 0 before incrementing.

questions.question: Why is unordered_map especially common in DSA and coding interviews?
questions.answer: Many problems require fast lookups or frequency counts, and unordered_map provides average constant-time access, making patterns like duplicate detection and frequency counting efficient and concise.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: unordered_map stores key-value pairs with unique keys, using hashing to achieve average O(1) insertion and lookup, with no ordering guarantee — the counterpart to map the same way unordered_set is to set. Its most iconic use in DSA is frequency counting: freq[c]++ for each element in a collection works because [] automatically creates a missing key with a default value of 0 before incrementing it, turning a common counting task into a single line. Because so many DSA and interview problems boil down to fast lookups or counting occurrences, unordered_map is one of the most heavily used STL containers in practice.

sections.title: Trick to Remember unordered_map
sections.text: unordered_map = a tally counter at a turnstile. New ID → starts at 0 automatically. freq[c]++ = the classic one-line frequency counter.
